content:
# Disagreement Log — Leviticus Chapter 25

**Chapter:** Leviticus 25 (Sabbatical Year and Jubilee)
**Arbiter:** SOPHIA
**Sources compared:** EMET (Literal), LOGOS (Formal), PNEUMA (Dynamic)
**Total disagreements:** 62

---

## Disagreement Index

| # | Verse(s) | Category | Issue |
|---|----------|----------|-------|
| 1 | 1 | Name form | "Moshe" vs. "Moses" |
| 2 | 1 | Structural | Presence/absence of "saying" |
| 3 | 2 | Rendering | Land-sabbath phrasing |
| 4 | 4 | Terminology | "Complete rest" vs. "solemn rest" for shabbaton |
| 5 | 4 | Structural | Conditional framing ("shall be" vs. "there shall be") |
| 6 | 5 | Terminology | "Untended" vs. "undressed" vs. "consecrated" for nezireikhah |
| 7 | 5 | Word choice | "What grows of itself" vs. "what grows on its own" |
| 8 | 6 | Structural | "Sabbath of the land shall be food" vs. "whatever the land produces" |
| 9 | 6 | Word choice | "Male servant/female servant" vs. "servants" |
| 10 | 8 | Word choice | "Count for yourself" vs. "Count off" |
| 11 | 8 | Terminology | "Weeks of years" vs. "Sabbath-years" |
| 12 | 9 | Terminology | "Ram's horn blast" vs. "loud trumpet" vs. "ram's horn" |
| 13 | 10 | Word choice | "Clan" vs. "family" for mishpachah |
| 14 | 10 | Structural | "Each of you shall return" vs. "every one of you is to return" |
| 15 | 11 | Terminology | "Untended vines" vs. "undressed vines" (repeated) |
| 16 | 12 | Word choice | "Produce of the field" vs. "what the fields produce on their own" |
| 17 | 13 | Structural | Paragraph break placement |
| 18 | 14 | Word choice | "Sell anything to your neighbor" vs. "make a sale to your neighbor" vs. "sell land to your neighbor" |
| 19 | 14 | Word choice | "Neighbor" vs. "fellow" for amitekha |
| 20 | 15 | Structural | Word order: price-setting clause |
| 21 | 18 | Structural | "And you shall do" vs. "Therefore you shall do" vs. "Follow my decrees" |
| 22 | 18 | Terminology | "Statutes and rules" vs. "statutes and laws" vs. "decrees and laws" |
| 23 | 20 | Structural | "And if you say" vs. "You may wonder" |
| 24 | 21 | Word choice | "Command my blessing" vs. "send my blessing" |
| 25 | 22 | Structural | Structure of the three-year provision sentence |
| 26 | 23 | Word choice | "Sojourners and settlers" vs. "strangers and sojourners" |
| 27 | 23 | Word choice | Addition of "only" (PNEUMA) |
| 28 | 24 | Word choice | "Land of your holding" vs. "country you possess" vs. "land that you hold" |
| 29 | 25 | Terminology | "Kinsman-redeemer" vs. "nearest redeemer" vs. "redeemer" |
| 30 | 26 | Word choice | "His hand attains" vs. "becomes prosperous and finds sufficient means" |
| 31 | 27 | Word choice | "Repay the balance" vs. "refund the difference" vs. "return the excess" |
| 32 | 28 | Word choice | "Released" vs. "go out" for yatsa |
| 33 | 30 | Word choice | "Pass in perpetuity" vs. "made permanent property" vs. "becomes permanent possession" |
| 34 | 31 | Word choice | "Open fields of the land" vs. "fields of the land" vs. "open fields" |
| 35 | 32 | Word choice | "Permanent right of redemption" vs. "may redeem at any time" vs. "always retain the right to redeem" |
| 36 | 33 | Structural | Interpretation of the ambiguous conditional clause |
| 37 | 33 | Word choice | "Holding among the sons of Israel" vs. "possession among the sons of Israel" |
| 38 | 34 | Word choice | "Open land" vs. "fields of pastureland" vs. "pastureland" |
| 39 | 35 | Structural | "As a sojourner and a settler" vs. "as though he were a sojourner and a resident alien" vs. "as you would a foreigner or sojourner" |
| 40 | 35 | Word choice | "Support himself with you" vs. "maintain himself with you" |
| 41 | 36 | Terminology | "Interest or profit" (two terms) vs. "interest of any kind" (one term) |
| 42 | 39 | Structural | "Make him serve as a slave" vs. "work as a slave" |
| 43 | 40 | Terminology | "Settler" vs. "sojourner" for toshav |
| 44 | 41 | Word choice | "Clan" vs. "family" for mishpachah (repeated) |
| 45 | 41 | Word choice | "Property of his fathers" vs. "possession of his fathers" vs. "property of his ancestors" |
| 46 | 42 | Structural | "For they are my servants" vs. "Because they are my servants" |
| 47 | 45 | Word choice | "Sons of the settlers" vs. "sojourners" vs. "foreigners residing" |
| 48 | 46 | Word choice | "Permanent holding" vs. "forever" vs. "permanently" for achuzah le-olam |
| 49 | 47 | Word choice | "Sojourner or settler" vs. "sojourner or resident alien" vs. "foreigner or sojourner" |
| 50 | 47 | Word choice | "Becomes prosperous" vs. "becomes rich" vs. "becomes wealthy" |
| 51 | 48 | Structural | "Retains the right of redemption" vs. "may be redeemed" vs. "redemption shall remain for him" |
| 52 | 49 | Word choice | "Uncle's son" vs. "cousin" for ben-dodo |
| 53 | 50 | Word choice | "Reckoned" vs. "rated" for the hired-worker calculation |
| 54 | 51 | Structural | "In proportion to them he shall return" vs. "he shall pay proportionately" vs. "he must repay a larger share" |
| 55 | 53 | Structural | Subject of the hired-worker clause ("he shall be with him" vs. "treat him as") |
| 56 | 54 | Word choice | "Go out" vs. "be released" for yatsa (persons) |
| 57 | 55 | Structural | "For it is to me" vs. "For the Israelites belong to me" |
| 58 | 55 | Name form | "Sons of Israel" vs. "Israelites" |
| 59 | 4, 5 | Consistency | "Complete rest" vs. "solemn rest" repeated |
| 60 | 43, 46, 53 | Consistency | All three agree on "ruthlessly" — but PNEUMA omits adverb in v. 46 |
| 61 | 10, 41 | Consistency | "Clan" vs. "family" for mishpachah in Jubilee contexts |
| 62 | 28, 54 | Consistency | "Released" vs. "go out" for yatsa: property vs. persons |

---

## Detailed Disagreement Records

**#1 — V. 1: "Moshe" vs. "Moses"**
- EMET: "spoke to Moshe on Mount Sinai"
- LOGOS: "spoke to Moses on Mount Sinai, saying"
- PNEUMA: "spoke to Moses on Mount Sinai"
- Ruling: "Moses" — English name forms per project convention throughout.

---

**#2 — V. 1: Presence/absence of "saying" (lemor)**
- EMET: includes "saying"
- LOGOS: includes "saying"
- PNEUMA: omits "saying" (begins direct discourse immediately)
- Ruling: "saying" retained — *lemor* is in the MT and serves as the standard speech-introduction formula in Leviticus.

---

**#3 — V. 2: Phrasing of the land-sabbath command**
- EMET: "the land shall sabbath a sabbath to the LORD"
- LOGOS: "the land shall keep a Sabbath to the LORD"
- PNEUMA: "the land itself must observe a Sabbath rest — a Sabbath belonging to the LORD"
- Ruling: LOGOS — "the land shall keep a Sabbath to the LORD." EMET's double-sabbath ("sabbath a sabbath") is woodenly literal for the cognate accusative *shavetah shabbat*. PNEUMA's "must observe" introduces an obligation-modal and adds an em-dash explanatory clause. LOGOS follows the grammar cleanly.

---

**#4 — V. 4 (and v. 5): "Complete rest" vs. "solemn rest" for shabbat shabbaton**
- EMET: "Sabbath of complete rest"
- LOGOS: "Sabbath of solemn rest"
- PNEUMA: "complete Sabbath rest"
- Ruling: "Sabbath of complete rest" — EMET. The intensified doubled form *shabbat shabbaton* conveys completeness/totality of rest rather than solemnity. "Solemn" connotes ceremonial gravity; "complete" captures the superlative force of the doubling. PNEUMA's reordering of the genitive to an adjective-noun compound loses the Hebrew construction.

---

**#5 — V. 4: Conditional framing**
- EMET: "the seventh year shall be a Sabbath of complete rest"
- LOGOS: "in the seventh year there shall be a Sabbath of solemn rest"
- PNEUMA: "the seventh year is to be a complete Sabbath rest"
- Ruling: "the seventh year shall be a Sabbath of complete rest" — EMET/adjusted. Direct attribution to the year is cleaner than LOGOS's existential "there shall be." PNEUMA's "is to be" is a modal construction.

---

**#6 — V. 5 (and v. 11): "Untended" vs. "undressed" vs. "consecrated" for nezireikhah**
- EMET: "your consecrated vine" (preserving the *nazir* cognate connection)
- LOGOS: "undressed vine" / "undressed vines"
- PNEUMA: "untended vines" / "untended vines"
- Ruling: "untended vine/vines" — PNEUMA. The Hebrew *nezireikhah* is from *nazir* ("separated/consecrated"), referring to a vine left unpruned and wild. EMET's "consecrated" is etymologically accurate but risks confusion with the Nazirite vow and a positive religious connotation, when the text means simply a vine left uncultivated. LOGOS's "undressed" is traditional (ESV, KJV) but archaic. PNEUMA's "untended" is clear and accurate for modern readers.

---

**#7 — V. 5: "What grows of itself" vs. "what grows on its own"**
- EMET: "what grows of its own accord from your harvest"
- LOGOS: "What grows of itself from your harvest"
- PNEUMA: "What grows on its own from last year's harvest"
- Ruling: "What grows of itself from your harvest" — LOGOS. The technical term *sefiach* = self-germinating grain from fallen seed. "Of itself" captures *sefiach* well. PNEUMA's "last year's harvest" is an interpretation added beyond the MT. "Of its own accord" (EMET) is wordy.

---

**#8 — V. 6: Structure of the sabbath-food provision**
- EMET: "And the sabbath of the land shall be food for you: for you and for your male servant and for your female servant..."
- LOGOS: "The Sabbath of the land shall be food for you: for you and for your male servant and for your female servant..."
- PNEUMA: "But whatever the land produces during its Sabbath year, you and your servants and your hired hands and the foreigners staying with you may eat it"
- Ruling: LOGOS — preserving the MT's colon-separated list structure (*ve-hayetah shabbat ha-aretz lakhem le-okhlah*) and the full list of beneficiaries. PNEUMA restructures the sentence substantially, collapses "male servant/female servant" to "servants," and replaces "sojourner" with "foreigners staying with you."

---

**#9 — V. 6: "Male servant/female servant" vs. "servants"**
- EMET: "your male servant and your female servant"
- LOGOS: "your male servant and your female servant"
- PNEUMA: "your servants"
- Ruling: "your male servant and your female servant" — EMET/LOGOS. The MT distinguishes *avdekha* (male servant) and *amatkha* (female servant). The distinction is present in the Hebrew and should be preserved; PNEUMA's collapse to "servants" loses the gender specificity.

---

**#10 — V. 8: "Count for yourself" vs. "count off"**
- EMET: "And you shall count for yourself"
- LOGOS: "You shall count seven weeks of years for yourself"
- PNEUMA: "Count off seven Sabbath-years"
- Ruling: "And you shall count for yourself" — the *lekha* ("for yourself") is in the MT and is retained. The *vav* opening (*ve-safarta*) preserved. PNEUMA drops *lekha* and uses the imperative.

---

**#11 — V. 8: "Weeks of years" vs. "Sabbath-years"**
- EMET: "seven sabbaths of years" (*sheva shabbatot shanim*)
- LOGOS: "seven weeks of years"
- PNEUMA: "seven Sabbath-years"
- Ruling: "seven weeks of years" — LOGOS. The MT *sheva shabbatot shanim* ("seven sabbaths of years") treats each seven-year period as a unit parallel to a week. "Weeks of years" captures this parallel. "Sabbath-years" (PNEUMA) is a common modern rendering but loses the counting-unit analogy. EMET's "sabbaths of years" is more literal but less idiomatic.

---

**#12 — V. 9: "Ram's horn blast" vs. "loud trumpet" vs. "ram's horn"**
- EMET: "the ram's horn blast" (*shofar teru'ah*)
- LOGOS: "the loud trumpet"
- PNEUMA: "the ram's horn"
- Ruling: "the ram's horn blast" — EMET. The *shofar* is specifically a ram's horn, not a metal trumpet. LOGOS follows a formal-equivalence tradition (ESV "loud trumpet") but *shofar* should not be generalized to "trumpet." "Ram's horn" (PNEUMA) drops the *teru'ah* (blast/alarm) component. "Ram's horn blast" preserves both elements.

---

**#13 — V. 10 (and v. 41): "Clan" vs. "family" for mishpachah**
- EMET: "his family" (*mishpachto*)
- LOGOS: "his clan"
- PNEUMA: "his own family"
- Ruling: "his clan" — LOGOS. In the Jubilee legal context *mishpachah* refers to the extended clan-network (intermediate between household and tribe), and "clan" captures this social unit more precisely than "family," which in modern English typically means the immediate nuclear family. Applied consistently at vv. 10, 41.

---

**#14 — V. 10: "Each of you shall return" vs. "every one of you is to return"**
- EMET: "each man to his holding and each man to his family"
- LOGOS: "each of you shall return to his property and each of you shall return to his clan"
- PNEUMA: "every one of you is to return to his own property; every one of you is to return to his own family"
- Ruling: LOGOS. The MT *ish el-achuzato ve-ish el-mishpachto tashuvu* ("each man to his holding and each man to his clan you shall return") — LOGOS renders naturally. PNEUMA's semicolon breaks the clause and adds "own" twice. EMET's "holding" and "family" are noted.

---

**#15 — Vv. 5, 11: "Untended" vs. "undressed" — consistency issue**
See #6 above. Applied at both v. 5 and v. 11.

---

**#16 — V. 12: "Produce of the field" vs. "what the fields produce on their own"**
- EMET: "from the field you may eat its produce"
- LOGOS: "You may eat the produce of the field"
- PNEUMA: "You may eat only what the fields produce on their own"
- Ruling: LOGOS. PNEUMA adds "only" (not in MT) and "on their own" (interpretation). The MT *mi-sadeh tochlu et-tevu'atah* = "from the field you may eat its produce" — LOGOS renders cleanly.

---

**#17 — V. 13: Paragraph break**
- EMET: groups v. 13 with vv. 14ff
- LOGOS: paragraph break before v. 13
- PNEUMA: paragraph break before v. 13
- Ruling: paragraph break before v. 13 — LOGOS/PNEUMA. Verse 13 reiterates the Jubilee-return principle as a heading for the property-sale legislation that follows.

---

**#18 — V. 14: "Sell anything" vs. "make a sale" vs. "sell land"**
- EMET: "when you sell a sale to your fellow" (literal: *timkeru mimkar*)
- LOGOS: "if you make a sale to your neighbor or buy from your neighbor"
- PNEUMA: "if you sell land to your neighbor or buy land from him"
- Ruling: "when you sell anything to your neighbor or buy from your neighbor" — synthesizing EMET's sense (any commercial transaction, not land-specific) with natural English. PNEUMA's "land" is an interpretation; the MT *mimkar* = "a sale/merchandise" is not restricted to land at this point.

---

**#19 — V. 14: "Neighbor" vs. "fellow" for amitekha**
- EMET: "fellow" (*amitekha* = associate/fellow Israelite)
- LOGOS: "neighbor"
- PNEUMA: "neighbor"
- Ruling: "neighbor" — LOGOS/PNEUMA. In the commercial context "neighbor" is the more natural English equivalent of *amith*. The root *amith* is from *am* (people) and means "associate/companion/fellow," but "neighbor" carries the required covenantal proximity.

---

**#20 — V. 15: Word order in price-setting clause**
- EMET: "According to the number of years after the jubilee you shall buy from your fellow"
- LOGOS: "You shall pay your neighbor according to the number of years after the Jubilee, and he shall sell to you according to the number of years for crops"
- PNEUMA: "You are to buy from your neighbor according to the number of years since the last Jubilee, and he is to sell to you according to the number of remaining years of harvests"
- Ruling: Consensus preserves the MT's two-clause symmetry (buying clause, then selling clause). LOGOS's "pay" substitutes for "buy" — the MT has *tikneh* ("you shall buy"), not "pay." Corrected in consensus to "you shall buy."

---

**#21 — V. 18: "And you shall do" vs. "Therefore you shall do" vs. "Follow"**
- EMET: "And you shall do my statutes"
- LOGOS: "Therefore you shall do my statutes and keep my rules and perform them"
- PNEUMA: "Follow my decrees and be careful to keep my laws"
- Ruling: "And you shall do my statutes and keep my rules and do them" — EMET/LOGOS. The MT *va-asitem et-chuqqotai ve-et mishpatai tishmeru va-asitem otam* — "and you shall do my statutes and my rules you shall keep and do them." The *vav* is coordinating, not inferential; LOGOS's "therefore" imports a consequence-logic not in the MT's waw. PNEUMA's "Follow/be careful" are paraphrases. "Do" is used twice in the MT (*asitem... va-asitem otam*) and is preserved.

---

**#22 — V. 18: "Statutes and rules" vs. "decrees and laws"**
- EMET: "my statutes and my rules"
- LOGOS: "my statutes and keep my rules"
- PNEUMA: "my decrees... my laws"
- Ruling: "statutes and rules" — EMET/LOGOS. Per project convention (from Lev 20): *chuqqotai* = "statutes," *mishpatai* = "rules." PNEUMA's "decrees" and "laws" are alternatives but depart from established terminology.

---

**#23 — V. 20: "And if you say" vs. "You may wonder"**
- EMET: "And if you say"
- LOGOS: "And if you say"
- PNEUMA: "You may wonder"
- Ruling: "And if you say" — EMET/LOGOS. The MT *ve-ki tomar'u* is a second-person plural conditional indicative, not a hypothetical wondering. PNEUMA's "may wonder" softens the directness and changes the speech act from quoted speech to internal reflection.

---

**#24 — V. 21: "Command my blessing" vs. "send my blessing"**
- EMET: "I will command my blessing upon you"
- LOGOS: "I will command my blessing on you"
- PNEUMA: "I will send my blessing on you"
- Ruling: "I will command my blessing on you" — EMET/LOGOS. The MT *ve-tsivviti et-birkhati* uses *tsiwwah* ("to command"), not a sending verb. YHWH commands his blessing as he commands his statutes — the same verb for divine decrees. PNEUMA's "send" replaces the distinctive command-verb with a neutral one.

---

**#25 — V. 22: Structure of the three-year provision sentence**
- EMET: "And you shall sow in the eighth year and eat from the old produce and until the ninth year until its produce comes in you shall eat the old produce"
- LOGOS: "When you sow in the eighth year, you will be eating some of the old crop; you shall eat the old until the ninth year, when its crop arrives"
- PNEUMA: "While you plant during the eighth year, you will eat from the old harvest and will continue to eat from it until the harvest of the ninth year comes in"
- Ruling: LOGOS structure, adjusted. The MT emphasizes continuing consumption of old stores through two calendar years. "Eating from the old crop" (LOGOS) captures the continuity. PNEUMA's "continue to eat from it" is accurate but adds "from it" beyond the MT. EMET's repetition of "old produce" follows the MT's structural repetition.

---

**#26 — V. 23: "Sojourners and settlers" vs. "strangers and sojourners"**
- EMET: "for sojourners and settlers you are with me"
- LOGOS: "For you are strangers and sojourners with me"
- PNEUMA: "you are only strangers and sojourners living here with me"
- Ruling: "For you are sojourners and settlers with me" — EMET/adjusted. The MT *ki-gerim ve-toshavim atem immadi* = "for sojourners and settlers you are with me." *Ger* = sojourner; *toshav* = settler/resident alien. These are two distinct terms and should not be merged into two near-synonyms ("strangers and sojourners," LOGOS). "Sojourners and settlers" preserves the distinction.

---

**#27 — V. 23: Addition of "only" (PNEUMA)**
- EMET: no "only"
- LOGOS: no "only"
- PNEUMA: "you are only strangers and sojourners"
- Ruling: "only" is not in the MT and is rejected. The addition softens the force by making Israel's tenant-status sound more limiting/modest than the MT's plain assertion.

---

**#28 — V. 24: "Land of your holding" vs. "country you possess" vs. "land that you hold"**
- EMET: "throughout all the land of your holding" (*u-ve-khol eretz achuzatekhem*)
- LOGOS: "in all the country you possess"
- PNEUMA: "throughout the land that you hold"
- Ruling: "throughout all the land of your holding" — EMET. The MT has *eretz achuzatekhem* with *achuzah* as the technical term for ancestral holding. "Holding" preserves this. LOGOS's "country" is too broad and drops *achuzah*. PNEUMA's verbal "that you hold" converts the legal noun to a participle. "All" (*kol*) is present in the MT and retained.

---

**#29 — V. 25: "Kinsman-redeemer" vs. "nearest redeemer" vs. "redeemer"**
- EMET: "his redeemer, the nearest one" (*go'alo ha-qarov*)
- LOGOS: "his nearest redeemer"
- PNEUMA: "his nearest kinsman-redeemer"
- Ruling: "his nearest kinsman-redeemer" — PNEUMA. At the first introduction of *go'el* in the chapter, the full compound "kinsman-redeemer" (per project terminology ruling) establishes the familial dimension of the institution. Subsequent uses in close proximity may abbreviate to "redeemer."

---

**#30 — V. 26: "His hand attains" vs. "becomes prosperous and finds sufficient means"**
- EMET: "his hand attains enough" (*ve-hissiggah yado*)
- LOGOS: "himself becomes prosperous and finds sufficient means to redeem it"
- PNEUMA: "he himself later prospers enough to buy it back"
- Ruling: LOGOS — "he himself becomes prosperous and finds sufficient means." The Hebrew idiom *nasag yad* ("the hand reaches/attains") = acquiring enough resources. LOGOS unpacks the idiom cleanly. PNEUMA's "buy it back" substitutes for "redeem it" (*lig'olo*), losing the *ga'al* vocabulary. EMET's literal "his hand attains" is obscure in English.

---

**#31 — V. 27: "Repay the balance" vs. "refund the difference" vs. "return the excess"**
- EMET: "return the excess to the man" (*ve-shillem et-ha'odeph*)
- LOGOS: "pay back the balance to the man"
- PNEUMA: "refund the difference to the man he sold it to"
- Ruling: "repay the balance" — LOGOS. *Ha'odeph* = "the surplus/excess/remainder." "Balance" (LOGOS) is the standard financial term for the remaining credit owed. "Difference" (PNEUMA) is also acceptable but slightly less precise in property-sale contexts. "Excess" (EMET) is accurate but less idiomatic.

---

**#32 — V. 28: "Released" vs. "go out" for yatsa (property)**
- EMET: "it shall go out in the jubilee" (*ve-yatsa ba-yovel*)
- LOGOS: "In the Jubilee it shall be released"
- PNEUMA: "It will be returned in the Jubilee"
- Ruling: "it shall be released in the Jubilee" — LOGOS, for property. The verb *yatsa* ("to go out") is used for both property release and personal release; for property "released" is the conventional English legal term. For persons (vv. 41, 54) "go out" is preferred to preserve the Exodus-resonant verb. PNEUMA's "returned" implies the land goes back to the seller, which is true but not the precise semantic of *yatsa*.

---

**#33 — V. 30: "Pass in perpetuity" vs. "made permanent property" vs. "becomes permanent possession"**
- EMET: "shall be established to the buyer in perpetuity" (*ve-qam ha-bayit li-tsemitut la-qoneh*)
- LOGOS: "shall be made permanent property of the buyer throughout his generations"
- PNEUMA: "becomes the permanent possession of the buyer and his descendants"
- Ruling: "shall pass in perpetuity to the buyer throughout his generations" — synthesizing. *Qam* = "to stand/rise/be established" (of legal title taking hold); *li-tsemitut* = "in perpetuity/permanently." "Pass in perpetuity" captures the title-transfer finality.

---

**#34 — V. 31: "Open fields of the land" vs. "fields of the land"**
- EMET: "upon the open land of the field" (*al sdeh ha-aretz*)
- LOGOS: "with the fields of the land"
- PNEUMA: "like open fields"
- Ruling: "classified with the open fields of the land" — EMET/adjusted. *Sdeh ha-aretz* = "the open field/land" outside walled cities. "Open fields" better conveys the unrestricted, fieldland character of these properties than bare "fields."

---

**#35 — V. 32: "Permanent right of redemption" vs. "may redeem at any time" vs. "always retain the right"**
- EMET: "shall have a perpetual right of redemption" (*ge'ulat olam*: redemption + forever)
- LOGOS: "may redeem at any time"
- PNEUMA: "always retain the right to redeem"
- Ruling: "shall have a permanent right of redemption" — EMET/adjusted. The MT *ge'ulat olam tihyeh la-Leviyyim* = "there shall be a perpetual redemption to the Levites." "Permanent right of redemption" preserves both *olam* (permanent/eternal) and *ge'ulah* (redemption). LOGOS's "may redeem at any time" renders the sense correctly but loses the *olam* emphasis. PNEUMA's "always retain" is a fair paraphrase.

---

**#36 — V. 33: Interpretation of the ambiguous conditional clause**
- EMET: "And if someone redeems from the Levites" — redemption by an outsider of Levite-sold property
- LOGOS: "if one of the Levites exercises his right of redemption" — a Levite redeeming his own property
- PNEUMA: "If a Levite redeems a house in a Levitical town — or if it is not redeemed" — hybrid reading
- Ruling: The Hebrew *va-asher yig'al min-ha-Leviyyim* is grammatically ambiguous: "whoever redeems from the Levites" or "if one redeems from among the Levites." The broader context (Levite cities never leave Levite hands permanently) supports the EMET/LOGOS sense: whether a Levite or outsider redeems, the property reverts at Jubilee. Consensus adopts EMET's syntax ("if one of the Levites redeems") to maintain the Jubilee-reversion principle.

---

**#37 — V. 33: "Holding among the sons of Israel" vs. "possession"**
- EMET: "their holding among the sons of Yisra'el"
- LOGOS: "their possession among the sons of Israel"
- PNEUMA: "their holding among the Israelites"
- Ruling: "their holding among the sons of Israel" — EMET. "Holding" for *achuzah* per project convention. "Sons of Israel" over "Israelites" per project convention.

---

**#38 — V. 34: "Open land" vs. "fields of pastureland" vs. "pastureland"**
- EMET: "the field of the open land of their cities" (*u-sdeh migrash arehhem*)
- LOGOS: "the fields of pastureland belonging to their cities"
- PNEUMA: "the pastureland belonging to their towns"
- Ruling: "the open land belonging to their cities" — EMET/adjusted. *Migrash* = the open/common land surrounding a city (cf. Num 35:2–5), used for Levite cattle-grazing but technically "commons/open land" rather than designated "pastureland." "Open land" is more precise than "pastureland" while being accessible. "Towns" (PNEUMA) is an acceptable rendering of *arehhem* ("their cities") but "cities" is the project convention.

---

**#39 — V. 35: "As a sojourner and a settler" vs. "as though he were a sojourner and a resident alien"**
- EMET: "as a sojourner and as a settler" (*ke-ger ve-khaToshav*)
- LOGOS: "as though he were a sojourner and a resident alien"
- PNEUMA: "as you would a foreigner or sojourner"
- Ruling: "as a sojourner and a settler" — EMET. The MT *ke-ger ve-khaToshav* = "as a sojourner and as a settler." LOGOS's "as though he were" introduces a comparative distancing not in the Hebrew — the command is direct, not hypothetical. PNEUMA reverses the term order, uses "foreigner" for *ger* (the weaker term), and drops *toshav* entirely. "Sojourner and settler" preserves the established paired terms.

---

**#40 — V. 35: "Support himself" vs. "maintain himself"**
- EMET: "his hand falters beside you"
- LOGOS: "cannot maintain himself with you"
- PNEUMA: "cannot support himself among you"
- Ruling: "cannot support himself with you" — PNEUMA/adjusted. The idiom *matah yado* ("his hand sinks/totters") means inability to sustain oneself economically. "Support himself" (PNEUMA) is slightly more natural than "maintain himself" (LOGOS) while retaining the same meaning. "With you" (*immakh*) is retained from LOGOS; PNEUMA's "among you" is also possible but "with you" is the literal *immakh*.

---

**#41 — V. 36: "Interest or profit" (two terms) vs. "interest of any kind" (one term)**
- EMET: "interest or profit" (*neshekh ve-tarbit*)
- LOGOS: "interest... or profit"
- PNEUMA: "interest of any kind"
- Ruling: "interest or profit" — EMET/LOGOS. The MT distinguishes *neshekh* ("bite"/interest on money) and *tarbit* ("increase"/profit on goods). These are two distinct prohibited practices with a specific Hebrew term each. PNEUMA's collapse to "interest of any kind" eliminates the second term entirely.

---

**#42 — V. 39: "Make him serve as a slave" vs. "work as a slave"**
- EMET: "you shall not work with him the work of a slave" (*lo ta'avod bo avodat eved*)
- LOGOS: "you shall not make him serve as a slave"
- PNEUMA: "do not make him work as a slave"
- Ruling: LOGOS — "you shall not make him serve as a slave." The MT uses the causative structure (*ta'avod bo* = "you shall cause him to work" the work of a slave). LOGOS renders this as "make him serve" — the causative is captured. PNEUMA's "do not make him work" is equivalent but less formal.

---

**#43 — V. 40: "Settler" vs. "sojourner" for toshav**
- EMET: "like a settler" (*ke-toshav*)
- LOGOS: "as a sojourner" (using "sojourner" for *toshav*, conflating with *ger*)
- PNEUMA: "as a sojourner living with you"
- Ruling: "as a settler" — EMET. The MT has *ke-sakhir ke-toshav* — "as a hired worker, as a settler." LOGOS renders *toshav* as "sojourner" here, conflating it with *ger*. The project has established "sojourner" for *ger* and "settler" for *toshav* as distinct terms. LOGOS's conflation is inconsistent with v. 35 where LOGOS itself distinguishes them ("sojourner and a resident alien"). Consensus: "settler" for *toshav* throughout.

---

**#44 — V. 41 (and v. 10): "Clan" vs. "family" for mishpachah**
See #13 above. Applied also at v. 41.

---

**#45 — V. 41: "Property of his fathers" vs. "possession of his fathers" vs. "property of his ancestors"**
- EMET: "the holding of his fathers"
- LOGOS: "the possession of his fathers"
- PNEUMA: "the property of his ancestors"
- Ruling: "the property of his fathers" — following the MT *achuzat avotav* ("the holding/property of his fathers"). "Fathers" (EMET/LOGOS) is literal and preserves the ancestral/patrilineal character. PNEUMA's "ancestors" is a slightly broader term. "Property" over "holding" here for natural English flow.

---

**#46 — V. 42: "For they are" vs. "Because they are"**
- EMET: "for they are my servants"
- LOGOS: "For they are my servants"
- PNEUMA: "Because they are my servants"
- Ruling: "For they are my servants" — EMET/LOGOS. The MT *ki avadai hem* = "for they are my servants." "For" is the causal connector (*ki*); "Because" (PNEUMA) is acceptable but slightly stronger than the MT's explanatory *ki*. "For" is more consistent with project convention for *ki* in theologically grounding clauses.

---

**#47 — V. 45: "Sons of the settlers" vs. "sojourners" vs. "foreigners residing"**
- EMET: "from the sons of the settlers" (*mi-benei ha-toshavim*)
- LOGOS: "from among the sojourners who are temporary residents among you" (conflating *toshavim* with *gerim*)
- PNEUMA: "from the foreigners residing among you"
- Ruling: "from among the sons of the settlers" — EMET. The MT specifically says *benei ha-toshavim* ("sons of the settlers") — the children of the *toshavim* (settler-class non-Israelites), not the *gerim* (sojourners). LOGOS conflates *toshav* with *ger* again. PNEUMA's "foreigners" is too broad.

---

**#48 — V. 46: "Permanent holding" vs. "forever" vs. "permanently" for achuzah le-olam**
- EMET: "a perpetual holding"
- LOGOS: "as a possession forever"
- PNEUMA: "make them slaves permanently"
- Ruling: "inherit as a permanent holding" — EMET/adjusted. The MT *la-reshet achuzah le-olam* = "to possess as a perpetual holding." "Permanent holding" captures both *achuzah* (the technical term) and *olam* (permanently). LOGOS's "forever" is a fair rendering of *olam* but drops *achuzah*. PNEUMA's "permanently" applies only to the slave status, not the *achuzah* concept.

---

**#49 — V. 47: "Sojourner or settler" vs. "sojourner or resident alien" vs. "foreigner or sojourner"**
- EMET: "a sojourner or settler with you" (*ger ve-toshav immakh*)
- LOGOS: "a sojourner or a resident alien with you"
- PNEUMA: "a foreigner or a sojourner living among you"
- Ruling: "a sojourner or settler with you" — EMET. The MT *ger ve-toshav* is the established paired term (cf. v. 23, 35). LOGOS's "resident alien" for *toshav* is an interpretive expansion. PNEUMA reverses the terms and uses "foreigner" for *ger*. The established project terms are "sojourner" (*ger*) and "settler" (*toshav*).

---

**#50 — V. 47: "Becomes prosperous" vs. "becomes rich" vs. "becomes wealthy"**
- EMET: "the hand of a sojourner or settler attains enough" (*tasig yad*)
- LOGOS: "becomes rich"
- PNEUMA: "becomes wealthy"
- Ruling: "becomes prosperous" — for consistency with v. 26's ruling on the same idiom (*nasag yad* / *tasig yad*). "Rich" and "wealthy" are sharper terms than the MT's economic-attainment idiom; "prosperous" captures the sense of achieving sufficient means without overstating.

---

**#51 — V. 48: "Retains the right of redemption" vs. "may be redeemed" vs. "redemption shall remain"**
- EMET: "redemption shall remain for him" (*ge'ulah tihyeh lo*)
- LOGOS: "he may be redeemed"
- PNEUMA: "he retains the right of redemption"
- Ruling: "he retains the right of redemption" — PNEUMA. The MT *ge'ulah tihyeh lo* = "redemption shall be his/he shall have [the right of] redemption." PNEUMA's "retains the right of redemption" captures the legal entitlement most clearly. LOGOS's passive "may be redeemed" omits the subject's legal standing. EMET's literal rendering is accurate but less idiomatic.

---

**#52 — V. 49: "Uncle's son" vs. "cousin" for ben-dodo**
- EMET: "his uncle's son" (*ben-dodo*)
- LOGOS: "his cousin"
- PNEUMA: "his cousin"
- Ruling: "his uncle's son" — EMET. In a legal kinship context, the precise relationship "uncle's son" (*ben-dodo* = son of the father's brother) is more transparent than "cousin," which in English covers a broad range of relationships (first, second, paternal, maternal). The law specifies the exact kinship degree and this should be preserved.

---

**#53 — V. 50: "Reckoned" vs. "rated" for the hired-worker calculation**
- EMET: "like the days of a hired worker it shall be with him"
- LOGOS: "shall be rated as the time of a hired worker"
- PNEUMA: "based on the rate paid to a hired hand for that number of years"
- Ruling: "shall be reckoned as the time of a hired worker" — LOGOS/adjusted. "Reckoned" is the standard term for accounting/calculation (cf. *chishav* in v. 50) and is more neutral than "rated." PNEUMA's paraphrase "based on the rate paid" restructures the sentence significantly.

---

**#54 — V. 51: Structure of redemption-proportion clause**
- EMET: "in proportion to them he shall return his redemption price from the sum he was purchased for"
- LOGOS: "he shall pay proportionately for his redemption some of his sale price"
- PNEUMA: "he must repay a larger share of the price he was purchased for"
- Ruling: "in proportion to them he shall return his redemption price from the sum he was purchased for" — EMET/adjusted. Preserves the proportionality principle (*le-fi hem*) and the source of the calculation (*kesef miqnato* = "the sum of his purchase price"). PNEUMA's "larger share" is an interpretation of when many years remain (v. 51 context), which is accurate but imports the contextual reasoning into the verse text.

---

**#55 — V. 53: Subject of the hired-worker clause**
- EMET: "like a hired worker year by year he shall be with him"
- LOGOS: "He shall treat him as a worker hired year by year"
- PNEUMA: "He is to be treated as a hired hand year by year"
- Ruling: "He shall be with him as a hired worker year by year" — EMET/adjusted. The MT *ki-sakhir shanah be-shanah yihyeh immo* = "as a hired worker year by year he shall be with him." The subject of *yihyeh* ("he shall be") is the enslaved person, not the owner. LOGOS makes the owner the subject ("He shall treat him"), which changes the sentence subject. PNEUMA's passive "is to be treated" is acceptable but less direct.

---

**#56 — V. 54: "Go out" vs. "be released" for yatsa (persons)**
- EMET: "he shall go out [*ve-yatsa*] in the year of jubilee"
- LOGOS: "shall be released in the year of Jubilee"
- PNEUMA: "are to be released in the Year of Jubilee"
- Ruling: "shall go out in the year of Jubilee" — EMET. For persons, the verb *yatsa* ("to go out") is the Exodus-resonant liberation verb (cf. *hotsethi* in vv. 38, 42, 55 — the Exodus-formula). The Jubilee liberation of enslaved Israelites is explicitly patterned on the Exodus (*hotsethi etkhem me-Mitsrayim*), and *yatsa* is the movement-verb in every case of Exodus liberation. Rendering it "go out" here preserves this theological resonance. "Released" (LOGOS/PNEUMA) is accurate but theologically flatter.

---

**#57 — V. 55: "For it is to me" vs. "For the Israelites belong to me"**
- EMET: "For to me the sons of Yisra'el are servants"
- LOGOS: "For it is to me that the sons of Israel are servants"
- PNEUMA: "For the Israelites belong to me as servants"
- Ruling: LOGOS — "For it is to me that the sons of Israel are servants." The MT *ki-li benei Yisra'el avadim* = "for to me the sons of Israel are servants." LOGOS's fronting of "to me" (*ki-li*) preserves the emphatic parallel with v. 23's *ki-li ha-aretz* ("for the land is mine"). PNEUMA's "belong to me" softens the explicit servant-language (*avadim*) and loses the *ki-li* structural parallel.

---

**#58 — V. 55: "Sons of Israel" vs. "Israelites"**
- EMET: "sons of Yisra'el" (with transliteration)
- LOGOS: "sons of Israel"
- PNEUMA: "the Israelites"
- Ruling: "sons of Israel" — LOGOS. Per project convention: *bney yisrael* = "sons of Israel" throughout. PNEUMA's "the Israelites" is a legitimate abbreviated rendering but diverges from the established project standard.

---

**#59 — Vv. 4, 5: "Complete rest" vs. "solemn rest" — consistency (see #4)**
The same *shabbat shabbaton* construction occurs in both vv. 4 and 5. LOGOS renders "solemn rest" in v. 4 and "solemn rest" in v. 5 — consistent but ruled against. EMET renders "complete rest" consistently. Consensus: "complete rest" in both verses.

---

**#60 — V. 46: "Ruthlessly" — PNEUMA omission**
- EMET: "but over your brothers the sons of Israel you shall not rule, one over another, ruthlessly" (includes *be-farekh*)
- LOGOS: "over your brothers the sons of Israel you shall not rule, one over another, ruthlessly"
- PNEUMA: "But over your brothers, the Israelites, you must not rule over one another harshly"
- Ruling: LOGOS — "but over your brothers the sons of Israel you shall not rule, one over another, ruthlessly." PNEUMA uses "harshly" for *be-farekh* rather than the established "ruthlessly." While "harshly" is acceptable, "ruthlessly" is the project term for *be-farekh* (cf. vv. 43, 53). Consistency demands "ruthlessly" at all three occurrences. Also: "sons of Israel" over "Israelites" per project convention.

---

**#61 — Vv. 10, 41: Consistency of "clan" vs. "family" for mishpachah**
Both EMET and PNEUMA use "family" at both occurrences; LOGOS uses "clan" at both. Ruling: "clan" throughout for *mishpachah* in Jubilee legal sections. The consistency ruling is logged here as a single entry covering both instances.

---

**#62 — Vv. 28, 54: "Released" vs. "go out" for yatsa — property vs. persons**
The same verb *yatsa* is used for property release (v. 28) and personal release (v. 54). The consensus treats these differently: "released" for property (v. 28) following English legal convention; "go out" for persons (v. 41, 54) following the Exodus-echo. This is a structural interpretation ruling logged here to explain the intentional asymmetry.

---

*Disagreement log complete. Leviticus 25:1–55. 62 disagreements recorded across 55 verses.*
*SOPHIA — Leviticus 25 consensus and disagreement log completed.*
